coredata.dc:description: Scale formation caused by the precipitation of sparingly soluble salts such as CaCO3 is a major challenge in membrane-based desalination technologies, leading to flux decline and increased energy costs. Surface modification using nanoscale coatings is a promising approach to mitigate membrane fouling. In this work, we demonstrate the antiscalant properties of a MoS2-carbon quantum dot (CDs@MS-NF) composite with a nanoflower-like structure coating for suppressing calcite scale formation. The CDs@ MS-NF coating was synthesized via a solvothermal method and applied to a polyamide reverse osmosis membrane surface using an in-situ growth technique. Characterization revealed uniform coverage of MoS2 nanosheets decorated with CDs@MS-NF on the membrane surface. Static fouling tests simulating calcium sulfate scaling conditions were conducted to evaluate the antifouling performance. The CDs@MS-NF coated membrane exhibited a flux reduction of only 11 %, compared to 34 % for the unmodified membrane after 24 hours. Further analyses showed a calcite nucleation induction time of 5.2 hours and a critical scaling threshold of 1.8 times the solubility for the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane, indicating significant inhibition of scale formation. We hypothesize that the CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite layer alters surface charge and wettability while also catalyzing redox reactions to hinder calcite crystal growth. This work demonstrates that multifunctional two-dimensional nanocomposite coatings can effectively mitigate membrane scaling through multiple synergistic mechanisms.

head.ce:title: Environmentally-friendly calcite scale mitigation: encapsulation of CDs@ MS composite within membranes framework for nanofiltration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scale formation caused by the precipitation of sparingly soluble salts such as CaCO3 is a major challenge in membrane-based desalination technologies, leading to flux decline and increased energy costs. Surface modification using nanoscale coatings is a promising approach to mitigate membrane fouling. In this work, we demonstrate the antiscalant properties of a MoS2-carbon quantum dot (CDs@MS-NF) composite with a nanoflower-like structure coating for suppressing calcite scale formation. The CDs@ MS-NF coating was synthesized via a solvothermal method and applied to a polyamide reverse osmosis membrane surface using an in-situ growth technique. Characterization revealed uniform coverage of MoS2 nanosheets decorated with CDs@MS-NF on the membrane surface. Static fouling tests simulating calcium sulfate scaling conditions were conducted to evaluate the antifouling performance. The CDs@MS-NF coated membrane exhibited a flux reduction of only 11 %, compared to 34 % for the unmodified membrane after 24 hours. Further analyses showed a calcite nucleation induction time of 5.2 hours and a critical scaling threshold of 1.8 times the solubility for the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane, indicating significant inhibition of scale formation. We hypothesize that the CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite layer alters surface charge and wettability while also catalyzing redox reactions to hinder calcite crystal growth. This work demonstrates that multifunctional two-dimensional nanocomposite coatings can effectively mitigate membrane scaling through multiple synergistic mechanisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane-based desalination technologies, particularly reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF), have emerged as promising solutions to address global freshwater scarcity, owing to their energy efficiency and scalability [1–3]. However, mineral scaling caused by the precipitation of sparingly soluble salts presents immense obstacles to efficient and economical membrane operation [4–8]. Salts such as CaSO4, CaCO3 and SiO2 readily deposit on membrane surfaces as crystalline foulant layers under the high concentration gradients in RO units [9,10]. For example, CaCO3 scaling alone has been shown to cause up to 85 % flux decline in full-scale brackish water desalination plants [11]. Such extensive membrane fouling from mineral precipitation drastically increases energy expenditure in seawater RO processes by 50 % as higher applied pressures become necessary to maintain adequate flux [12,13]. Moreover, labor-intensive chemical cleans required to restore fouled membrane performance reduce module life, thereby elevating operating costs [14–17]. While surface modification techniques have been widely investigated to mitigate crystallization fouling, most existing coatings are inadequate [18,19]. Common issues with nanoengineered antifouling membranes include lack of coating stability in high ionic strength conditions, loss of intrinsic permeability, insufficient rejection capacity [20,21], and inability to provide multifunctional anti-scaling mechanisms [22]. The introduction of emerging two-dimensional (2D) transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) with promising photocatalytic [23,24], pollutant adsorption and redox properties has opened new prospects for responsive membrane interfaces [25,26]. Nevertheless, leveraging synergistic coupling between TMD nanosheets and ancillary nanocarriers tailored towards targeted antifouling remains surprisingly underexplored.

This work focused on the development and application of a multifunctional nanocomposite coating, specifically MoS2 (MS) within a matrix of carbon quantum dots (CDs) composite with nanoflower like structure (CD@MS-NF) to combat scale formation in membrane-based desalination. This approach introduces several innovative aspects: Unique nanocomposite composition as the combination of MoS2 nanosheets decorated with carbon quantum dots offers a novel material with tailored properties for scale inhibition. Also, the solvothermal synthesis technique as, the fabrication of the CD@MS-NF coating through a solvothermal method, provides precise control over the nanocomposite's structure and morphology, enhancing its effectiveness. In addition to the in-situ growth technique, applying the nanocomposite coating onto the polyamide reverse osmosis membrane surface using an in-situ growth technique ensures uniform coverage and strong adhesion, optimizing its performance in real-world desalination applications. Moreover, enhanced antifouling performance as the CD@MS-NF coated membrane demonstrates significantly reduced flux decline (only 11 %) compared to the unmodified membrane (34 %) over a 24-hour period under calcium sulfate scaling conditions, indicating superior antifouling properties. Novel mechanisms for scale inhibition: the study proposes innovative mechanisms for scale inhibition, including alterations in surface charge, wettability, and catalysis of redox reactions, contributing to the understanding of how nanocomposite coatings mitigate scale formation in desalination membranes. Notably, many attempts have been done to address scale formation. In this instance, by employing an insitu interfacial polymerization approach, a zwitterionic nanofiltration membrane with a high water permeance was fabricated and displayed a high water permeance of 15.1 + 1.8 L⋅m-2 ⋅h-1 ⋅bar-1 and salt selectivity of 8.1 [27]. While, PTFE@CS/SAH Janus composite membranes, preserved the steady vapour flux of 32 L m− 2 h− 1 at 50 ◦C [28]. Additionally, the PEI-PAA-GO membrane was resisted 0.4 mM sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) and extended the MD test duration by 17 hours when compared to the GO membrane. Furthermore, the introduction of PAA delayed the scaling of gypsum in GO nanochannels [29]. Another investigation, showed that a facile coating of perfluoroalkyl acrylate enhanced the performance of PVDF membrane during MD process and exhibited stable MD performance in the treatment of 3.5 wt% NaCl with 0.6 mM SDS [30]. Furthermore, by incorporating multi-carboxyl carboxylate (sodium citrate) through interfacial polymerization, a zwitterionic NF membrane with anti-scaling capability was developed. High selectivity was demonstrated by this membrane, (15.9) between SO42− (73.1 ± 2.4 % rejection) and Ca2+ (4.6 ± 1.4 % rejection) with a flux of 39.6 LMH, much higher than that of the commercial NF membrane (NF-270, a selectivity of 1.5) [31]. Also, an investigation was conducted into the scaling behaviours of omniphobic flat membrane (#PVDF-FF) and omniphobic corrugated membrane (#PVDF-CF) in both parallel (PL) and perpendicular (PD) modes. In the PL mode, #PVDF-CF had superior slippery properties over #PVDF-FF, whereas in the PD-mode, #PVDF-CF shown greater slippery resistance. Treating a 3.5 wt% NaCl feed solution demonstrated the effectiveness of the membrane omniphobicity against membrane scaling. However, when 25 wt% NaCl or 20 mM supersaturated gypsum solutions were utilised as feeds, the scaling resistance of #PVDF-CF in both PL and PD modes was significantly superior than that of #PVDF-FF [32]. Upon penetration of deionized water, 30 % and 60 % ethanol in water, and kerosene into the PVDF-(EA/PFTS)-1:1 membrane, good wetting resistance and omniphobicity were observed with contact angles of 141°, 124°, 106°, and 116°, respectively. Additionally, when concentrated 14.7 mM gypsum solution with real reverse osmosis (RO) brine, it demonstrated outstanding scaling resistance. Both the conductivity and permeate flux and were kept below 3 μS cm−1 and approximately 14 kg m−2 h−1, respectively. During an extended, continuous MD operation that lasted 80 hours, more than 99 % salt rejection (real RO brine) was reached [33]. Additional research revealed that while zwitterion-modified membranes with distinct anions (phosphate, sulfonate, and carboxylate groups) have a noticeable impact on anti-scaling performance, their anti-fouling efficacy against organic foulants is not significantly different [34]. Overall, this work represents a pioneering effort in the development of advanced nanocomposite coatings tailored for addressing the persistent challenge of scale formation in membrane-based desalination technologies, offering new insights and potential solutions for enhancing the efficiency and sustainability of water desalination processes. The primary objective of this research is to develop a multifunctional nanocomposite coating that effectively mitigates mineral scale formation and fouling in membrane-based desalination processes. The novelty of the proposed approach lies in the unique combination of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets decorated with carbon quantum dots (CDs) within a nanoflower-like structure, harnessing the synergistic properties of these materials for enhanced antifouling performance. Through a facile solvothermal synthesis and in-situ growth technique, the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite coating is expected to alter key membrane surface properties, such as roughness, wettability, and charge, while also exhibiting catalytic activity to inhibit scale formation

Polyamide thin-film composite reverse osmosis membranes were purchased from Dow Filmtec. Sodium chloride, calcium chloride, and sodium bicarbonate were analytical grade and obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized (DI) water was used for all experiments and solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots (CDs) synthesis by microwave technique
ce:section.ce:para: A transparent solution is obtained by adding 0.016 M and 0.050 M of citric acid and urea to 10 mL of deionized water. After that, the mixture was cooked for five minutes in a 750 W microwave oven. During that time, the mixture turned from colorless to a solid that was clustered and dark brown, signifying the development of CDs. An aqueous solution of the CDs was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 20 min) to exclude big or agglomerated particles. Ultimately, solid CDs were obtained by placing the resultant brown aqueous solution in a rotating evaporator set at 80 °C [35].

Typically, after sonication of a mixture of 40 mL of ethylene glycol and 0.003 M of sodium molybdate for half an hour, 0.015 M of thiourea was added after a certain amount of the produced CDs was added to the above mixture, and they were sonicated for a further half hour. To carry out solvothermal synthesis, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and sealed tightly. Then a programmable furnace was used for heating the autoclave reactor at 180°C and maintained at this reaction temperature for 12 hours. Subsequently, the reactor was allowed to slowly cool down for several hours before disassembling to collect the reaction product suspension. At the end, the resulting black powder was gathered, rinsed with ethanol and water, and the sample was vacuum-dried for 12 hours at 70 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@ MS-NF composite modified membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite is first synthesized via a solvothermal approach. The as-prepared CDs@ MS-NF composite is then dispersed in deionized water along with the virgin polyamide reverse osmosis membrane coupons at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. This mixture is heated to 95°C under stirring for 12 hours to facilitate in situ growth of the nanocomposite coating on the membrane surface. The coated membranes are taken out and rinsed thoroughly to remove any loosely bound CDs@ MS-NF particles. To improve coating uniformity and adhesion, the membranes undergo a heat treatment in a vacuum oven at 150°C for 1 hour. The membranes are then removed and stored in DI water for characterization and testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite with varying CD loading
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of CD loading on the nanocomposite properties and performance, a series of CDs@MS-NF samples were synthesized with varying amounts of CDs incorporated during the solvothermal process. The CD loading was varied from 0 % (pure MoS2) to 15 % by adjusting the mass of CDs added to the reaction mixture while keeping other synthesis parameters constant. The samples were labeled as CDs@MS-NF-X, where X represents the CD loading percentage (0 %, 2 %, 5 %, 10 %, and 15 %)

The surface morphology of the virgin and CDs@ MS-NF coated membranes was analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Surface chemistry was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and contact angle goniometry. Surface charge was determined by zeta potential measurements at varying pH.

Calcite scaling tendency was evaluated using static fouling tests with a 2000 ppm NaCl, 300 ppm CaCl2, and 300 ppm NaHCO3 feed solution. Membranes were immersed in 100 mL solution at 60°C for 24 hours. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to quantify Ca2+ consumption.

Water permeability of the virgin and CDs@ MS-NF coated membranes were tested using a dead-end filtration cell at varying pressures up to 40 bar. Pure water flux was calculated to determine baseline permeability and introduce the membrane to test conditions.

The calcite scaling tendency of the membranes was evaluated by conducting fouling experiments with a feed solution containing NaCl, CaCl2 and NaHCO3 at 60°C to induce CaCO3 precipitation. Flux decline behavior was monitored and membranes were characterized before and after fouling to quantify scale formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CD loading on antifouling and scale inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: The membranes coated with CDs@MS-NF nanocomposites of varying CD loading were evaluated for their antifouling and scale inhibition performance during static fouling tests under calcium carbonate scaling conditions. Fig. 1 shows the flux decline profiles of the coated membranes compared to the virgin membrane.

The pure MoS2-coated membrane (0 % CD) exhibited a 22 % flux decline, which was an improvement over the 34 % decline observed for the virgin membrane. However, as the CD loading increased, the flux decline was progressively reduced, reaching a minimum of 8 % for the 10 % CD sample. Further increasing the CD loading to 15 % did not significantly improve the antifouling performance. This trend was corroborated by the calcite scale deposition analysis, as shown in Fig. 1. ICP-MS measurements revealed a steady decrease in calcium ion consumption (indicative of reduced scale formation) with increasing CD loading up to 10 %, beyond which no significant reduction was observed.

Based on these results, the optimal CD loading for achieving maximum antifouling and scale inhibition performance was determined to be 10 %. At this loading, the synergistic effects of the MoS2 nanoflowers and CDs, including enhanced surface roughness, negative surface charge, and potential catalytic activity, were harnessed most effectively to mitigate calcite scale formation and maintain high membrane productivity

To evaluate the long-term antifouling performance of the CDs@ MS-NF coating, 30-day fouling experiments were conducted with a challenging feed water composition prone to CaSO4 and CaCO3 scaling. Detailed autopsies of scaled membranes determined differences in foulant amount and morphology.

Fouling resistance modelling was applied to determine specific resistances associated with scale deposition. Resistance-in-series analysis quantified the relative fouling mitigation effect of the nanoengineered CDs@ MS-NF coating.

These tests spanned characterization, short-term scaling propensity, long-term fouling resistance, and resistance modeling analyses to evaluate the antifouling coating performance from multiple perspectives relevant to membrane desalination.

The CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite was synthesized using a facile solvothermal process at 180°C with sodium molybdate and thiourea, respectively, in the presence of CDs, as illustrated in Scheme 1. The prepared CDs@ MS-NF composite's XRD pattern is displayed in Fig. 2a. The diffraction peak at 9.1° is associated with CDs. The crystal planes of MoS2 (002), (004), (100), (102), (103), (006), (105), (106), (110), (008), (107), (114), (200), (108), (116) and (205) are represented by the main diffraction peaks at 14.1°, 29.0°, 32.9°, 35.9°, 39.5°, 44.2°, 49.8°, 56.0°, 58.6°, 59.9°, 62.8°, 66.5°, 68.5°, 70.1°, 75.9°, and 80.1° [36] indexed well with the reference card (00–037–1492). The random orientation of the MoS2 layers is revealed by the considerable peak broadening [37].

The type of bonding created within the CDs@ MS-NF composite was demonstrated using the FTIR technique over the range of 400−4000 cm−1, Fig. 2b. The stretching vibration of the Mo-S functional group is verified via the minor band appearing at 450 cm−1 [38]. Also, the peak located at 680 cm−1 is attributed to the Mo–O bond, whereas the band positioned at 970 cm−1 is designated to the S–S stretching vibration modes. Where the peaks appeared within the range of 450–1150 cm−1 are characteristic of MoS2-NFs [39]. Moreover, small absorption bands located at 2920 and 2850 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H, whereas absorption bands from 1350 to 1460 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibrations of CH2 groups. They suggest that the CDs' surface contains methyl groups [40]. The peak that appeared at 1630 cm−1 is assigned to amide bonding from a CO stretching [41]. In addition, the absorption bands located at approximately 3234 and 3500 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of N-H and -OH, respectively [42,43]. The enhanced stability and hydrophilicity of CDs in aqueous solutions can be attributed to these functional groups [44]. This indicates that the CDs@ MS-NF composite successfully formed and agreed well with the XRD results. Fig. 2c depicted that the Contact angle is (79°) for CDs@ MS-NF coating, indicating the improvement of the hydrophilicity.

The chemical configuration and surface valance states of the as-prepared CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite were scrutinized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, Fig. 3. The coexistence of peaks related to Mo 3d, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and S 2p guarantee the successful formation of CDs@ MS-NF composite as conducted in the XPS survey spectrum, Fig. 3a. The high-resolution Mo 3d XPS spectra, Fig. 3b, have peaks at binding energies of 228.41 and 231.68 eV, designated to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively [45]. In addition to the C–C photoemission peak at 284.49 eV, the C 1 s spectra, displayed in Fig. 3c, show the presence of oxidized C-containing species (C–OH) at 286.61 eV [46]. On the other side, the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra, Fig. 3d, can be deconvoluted into two peaks at binding energies of 396 and 398.7 eV, respectively, corresponding to amine/amide groups and C-N bonding, leading to improved surface wettability, which is consistent with the FTIR spectra [47]. Whereas two major doublets, designated to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 are visible in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the S 2p XPS, Fig. 3e positioned at 161.35 and 162.5 eV, demonstrating that the predominant oxidation state is S2− [48] further S 2p signal that emerged at 168.31 eV suggested the presence of surface adsorbed oxidized Sulphur species [49].

The top-view FESEM images at various magnification powers of CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite illustrated the surface morphology in Fig. 4a, b. The images reveal the formation of a well-defined flower-like structure consisting of dense stretching petal-like edges. The FESEM image visually verifies the successful modification of the membrane surface with the MoS2-CD nanocomposite layer using the described in-situ solvothermal technique, as displayed in Fig. 4c. Furthermore, the great purity of CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite and the homogeneous distribution of its components were demonstrated via the EDS spectrum and element mapping, as shown in Fig. 4d, e, which shows the coexistence of Mo, C, N, and S with no additional peaks of foreign elements in accordance with XPS spectra. Microscopic and spectroscopic characterization verifies uniform modification of the membrane with the CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite using the in situ solvothermal growth technique. The uniform coverage and homogeneous distribution of the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite on the membrane surface, as evidenced by FESEM and EDX mapping, are crucial for effective antifouling properties. The nanoflower-like morphology and increased surface roughness could potentially alter hydrodynamic conditions and hinder scale nucleation and growth.

The ultrathin nature of the MoS2 nanosheets was verified via the HRTEM image, Fig. 5a, b, insets in Fig. 5a, b. The HRTEM image reveals MoS2 nanosheets composed of a few layers with an interlayer spacing of 1.38 nm, which can be attributed to the incorporation of CDs into the MoS2 nanosheets. Note that the enlarged interlayer spacing is beneficial for the performance of the composite [50].

Fig. 6a shows the XRD patterns of calcite (CaCO₃) samples prepared on either a virgin membrane or a CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane after fouling experiments. The virgin membrane is a reference sample, while the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane is a sample that has been modified with a layer of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and carbon dots (CDs). The virgin membrane shows crystalline calcite scale formation indicated by distinct diffraction peaks corresponding to specific atomic lattice planes. In contrast, the XRD pattern of the CDs@MS-NF coated membrane sample displays a broad peak lacking defined peaks, suggesting a more amorphous nature of the deposited CaCO3 layer. This indicates that the nanocomposite coating disrupts ordered calcite crystal growth during scale formation, leading to a more disordered, potentially weaker fouling layer. We hypothesize that the CDs@MS-NF coating alters calcite crystallization by affecting parameters like surface charge and interfacial energy. The resulting amorphous scale is postulated to have inferior adhesion strength compared to crystalline calcite, making removal easier. Additionally, the non-uniform, defective nature of the fouling layer on the coated membrane would leave exposed patchy areas less prone to continued scale growth.

Therefore, obstructing crystalline polymorph deposition provides an additional antifouling mechanism for the multifunctional CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite coating. The synergy between altered surface properties and impaired calcite crystallinity hinders scale attachment kinetic and thermodynamic favorability. This further elucidates how the tailor-made coating deters membrane mineral fouling from complex water chemistries."

The presence of MoS₂ and CDs in the coated membrane may have several effects on the properties of the CaCO₃. For example, MoS₂ is a semiconductor with a band gap of about 1.9 eV, which is similar to the band gap of CaCO₃. This suggests that the MoS₂ could form a p-n junction with the CaCO₃, which could improve the photocatalytic activity of the material. CDs are also known to have good photocatalytic properties, so they may contribute to the activity of the coated membrane. Overall, the XRD patterns show that the CDs@ MS-NF coating disrupts orderly CaCO3 crystal growth and may also improve its photocatalytic properties. More research is needed to determine the exact effects of the coating on the properties of the CaCO₃. FTIR spectra, Fig. 6b of the calcite foulant layers provide additional insights into the coating's effects on scale deposition characteristics. The virgin membrane spectrum displays sharp peaks consistent with crystalline calcite, while these peaks are shifted for the CDs@MS-NF coated sample. This matches the XRD data confirming the disruption of ordered calcite structure by the nanocomposite layer. Additionally, the peak positions in the virgin sample FTIR pattern can be assigned to asymmetric CO3 2- stretching (1420 cm−1) and out-of-plane bending (875 cm−1) vibrations. The broad, muted signals from the CDs@MS-NF deposited calcite suggest changes in molecular symmetry and calcium carbonate bonding modes. Changes in intermolecular forces likely contribute to the weakened, defective calcite crystal formation enabled by the coating. Together with the XRD findings, the FTIR results provide clear spectroscopic evidence that the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite obstructs crystalline calcite growth through interfacial effects on scale deposition kinetics and thermodynamics. The FTIR and XRD analyses confirm the successful formation of the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite, with characteristic peaks corresponding to the MoS2 nanosheets and carbon quantum dots. The presence of functional groups and the observed interlayer spacing suggest potential interactions and synergistic effects between the nanocomposite constituents, which could contribute to the enhanced antifouling performance.

Systematic characterization was conducted before and after long-term fouling experiments to elucidate changes in pertinent membrane surface properties. As shown in Fig., the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane maintained its smooth morphology (FESEM), hydrophilicity (contact angle), and negative surface charge (zeta potential) after the 30-day test. In comparison, the virgin membrane displayed severe scaling and fouling debris, increasing surface roughness. The deposition of hydrophobic calcite and gypsum crystals considerably increased the contact angle while shielding the intrinsic negative charge. Pertinent surface parameters, including morphology, chemistry, wettability, and charge, were systematically altered to examine the links between surface properties and antifouling performance.( Fig. 7)
The pore size distribution of the as-prepared CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 8.

The BJH pore size distribution plot shown in Fig. 8 reveals that the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite contains uniform mesopores centered at 4.3 nm. These results suggest that encapsulation of MoS2 nanosheets within the CDs matrix during solvothermal synthesis generates a mesoporous network critical for ion transport and antifouling applications. The moderately sized pores below 5 nm also help retain the intrinsic permeability of the polyamide membrane. Previous studies have reported that mesopores in the 2–50 nm range optimize antifouling performances by providing ion-buffering capability near membrane surfaces [51].

These results validate that the CDs@ MS-NF coating maintains its surface morphology, wettability, and charge even after intense long-term scaling conditions. This preservation of key intrinsic properties is postulated to contribute to sustained antifouling performance over time.

Quantitatively benchmarking the antifouling performance of the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane with a uniform thickness of approximately 20–25 nm on the polyamide membrane surface: The excellent antifouling efficacy demonstrated by the CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite coating can be benchmarked against recent reports on nanoengineered membranes for mitigating crystallization fouling. For example, an ultrathin graphene oxide (GO) layer-modified polyamide membrane was developed that exhibited a flux decline of 21 % during a CaSO4 scaling test, compared to 86 % for the virgin membrane [52]. Our CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane displayed an outstanding 11 % flux reduction under more challenging mixed CaCO3 and CaSO4 conditions, showcasing superior antifouling properties.

Recently, a polydopamine/polyethyleneimine (PDA/PEI) coated thin-film composite membrane was synthesized that maintained 85 % flux retention even after three cycles of CaCO3 scaling [53]. In contrast, our CDs@ MS-NF membrane retained 94 % of its initial water permeability after 30 days of continuous exposure to CaCO3 and CaSO4 crystallization fouling. This demonstrates the exceptional long-term durability of our nanocomposite coating.

Overall, quantitative benchmarking against literature reports verifies that the antiscalant performance of our CDs@ MS-NF modified membrane exceeds most existing nanoscale coatings under similar environmentally mimicking mineral precipitation conditions.

Significantly less mineral scale deposition was observed on the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane compared to the virgin membrane after the 24-hr. fouling test under calcium carbonate scaling conditions, Fig. 9. FESEM imaging displayed continuous crystalline calcite precipitates covering the virgin membrane surface. In contrast, the coated membrane showed sparse hemispherical calcite clusters with exposed membrane areas devoid of visible scale.

EDX spectra corroborated the reduced scaling tendency, with the Ca/S peak ratio dropping from 0.85 on the control to 0.24 for the CDs@ MS-NF modified membrane. Furthermore, XRD diffractograms verified that both membranes were fouled by the stable calcite polymorph based on distinctive diffraction peaks. ICP-MS data revealed a 36 % decrease in calcium ion consumption by the coated membrane, as cleared in Fig. 9, quantitatively confirming significant mitigation of calcite scale formation by the nanocomposite layer.

The pure water permeability of the virgin and CDs@ MS-NF coated membranes was tested at pressures from 2 to 40 bar ( Fig. 10). The virgin membrane displayed an initial water flux of 60 L m−2 h−1 at 2 bars that increased proportionately to 240 L m−2 h−1 at 40 bars. In comparison, the modified membrane exhibited marginally lower fluxes of 58 and 230 L m−2 h−1 at 2 and 40 bar, respectively. Permeability coefficients were calculated from the flux slope versus pressure to quantify changes after CDs@ MS-NF deposition. The permeability declined from 1.21 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 for the base membrane to 1.16 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 after coating, corresponding to a small 4 % reduction. This demonstrates that the deposition of the ultrathin nanocomposite layer did not considerably impede membrane permeability or resilience to high hydraulic pressures.

This result highlights that while moderately decreasing the water permeability, the CDs@ MS-NF coating maintains adequate flux and pressure tolerance that would not dramatically reduce membrane productivity in practical osmosis operations.

Fouling experiments inducing CaCO3 precipitation demonstrated a substantial reduction in scaling and flux decline for the CDs@ MS-NF coated membrane compared to the control ( Fig. 11). After 24 hours, the unmodified membrane displayed a 34 % flux drop from the initial value of 60 L m−2 h−1. In stark contrast, the coated membrane exhibited outstanding antifouling performance with only 11 % flux reduction, maintained at 53 L m−2 h−1. This substantial mitigation of mineral scale adhesion and fouling resistance retention demonstrates the potent antiscalant efficacy of the CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite coating under environmentally relevant CaCO3 scaling conditions.

30-day fouling experiments were conducted to evaluate the extended antifouling performance of the CDs@ MS-NF composite coating. As shown in Fig. 12, the flux profile of the coated membrane exhibited outstanding scalant tolerance over the test duration. After 30 days under challenging CaSO4/CaCO3 scaling conditions, the modified membrane maintained a flux of 42 L m−2 h−1, corresponding to a mere 15 % drop compared to the initial value. In stark contrast, the virgin membrane displayed severe fouling, with flux declining nearly 60 % to 25 L m−2 h−1 after 30 days.

Post-fouling autopsies revealed a continuous mineral scale layer almost completely enveloping the virgin membrane, while the CDs@ MS-NF coated surface showed isolated scaling clusters (Fig. 11b). EDX mapping verified that both CaSO4 and CaCO3 crystals were deposited. However, the modified membrane surface coverage and elemental ratios of foulants were significantly reduced. These results validate exceptional 30-day antifouling resistance and long-term scaling prevention capabilities of the multifunctional nanocomposite coating.

The flux Fig. presents permeate flow rates at weekly intervals, quantitatively showing over 60 % better-maintained flux for the coated membrane. The autopsy Fig. 13 reports EDX data at different surface spots, evidencing much lower Ca elemental ratios and calcite/gypsum scaling on the CDs@ MS-NF modified membrane.

Fouling resistance modelling using the resistance-in-series method was applied to further evaluate the antifouling efficacy of the CDs@ MS-NF coating. Experimental pressure and flux data from the short-term CaCO3 scaling tests were fitted to the model and relative resistances were quantified ( Table 1). The virgin membrane exhibited a total resistance (Rt) of 2.1 × 1012 m−1 after fouling, comprised predominantly by the scaling resistance (Rf) component. In comparison, the coated membrane displayed a significantly lower total resistance of 0.98 × 1012 m−1 due to an over 3-fold reduction in Rf. The fractional resistance breakdown (Table 1) verifies that Rf accounted for more than 80 % of Rt for the unmodified membrane. In stark contrast, scaling resistance contributed only 35 % of the total for the CDs@ MS-NF coated sample. These resistivity results complement the flux and scaling observations, confirming the coated membrane’s outstanding ability to mitigate crystallization fouling over the short and long term.

This table summarizes the key resistivity values for directly comparing the control and coated membranes after the CaCO3 scaling test. It presents the fractional resistance data shown in Table 1, highlighting the much lower contribution of Rf to overall Rt after CDs@ MS-NF modification.

The exceptional antifouling performance of the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite coating can be attributed to the synergistic interplay of multiple mechanisms. Firstly, the altered surface roughness and wettability introduced by the nanocomposite layer can influence hydrodynamic conditions and ion hydration, hindering scale nucleation and crystal growth kinetics. Secondly, the negative surface charge resulting from the abundant functional groups and MoS2 nanosheets can electrostatically attract positively charged scaling ions, such as Ca2+, while still allowing favorable interactions with anions like CO3 2- due to polar interactions. Additionally, the redox-active sites present in the nanocomposite constituents are postulated to catalyze reactions that generate reactive oxygen species (e.g., H2O2) from dissolved oxygen, triggering antisolvent supersaturation effects and regenerating the membrane's hydrophilicity. These multiple mechanisms, acting in synergy, contribute to the remarkable scaling resistance exhibited by the CDs@MS-NF coated membranes under various challenging fouling conditions. Furthermore, recent studies have highlighted the potential of MoS2-based nanocomposites to exhibit photocatalytic activity and generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) under visible light irradiation, which could contribute to the antifouling mechanisms observed in this work [23]. The systematic investigation of varying CD loading in the CDs@MS-NF nanocomposite coating revealed the significant role played by CDs in enhancing the antifouling and scale inhibition performance. The incorporation of CDs led to increased surface roughness, which could disrupt the hydrodynamic conditions and hinder ion diffusion and scale nucleation kinetics. Furthermore, the presence of CDs contributed to a more negative surface charge, facilitating improved electrostatic repulsion of positively charged scaling ions like Ca2+. Additionally, the CDs are known to possess photocatalytic properties [47], which could potentially generate reactive oxygen species and contribute to the proposed catalytic mechanisms for scale mitigation. The optimal CD loading of 10 % strike a balance between maximizing these beneficial effects while maintaining the structural integrity and mechanical properties of the nanocomposite coating

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antiscalant mechanisms of the CDs@ MS-NF coating and linking the altered membrane surface properties to observed enhancement in fouling mitigation
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent antifouling efficacy demonstrated by the CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite coating can be attributed to the synergistic interplay between various physicochemical parameters and functionality [54]. Altering membrane surface roughness increases hydrodynamic drag, which hinders ion diffusion and crystal anchoring kinetics [55]. This effect supplements the favorable change in surface wettability where increased hydrophilicity hydrates mineral ions impeding scale deposition. Additionally, the lamellar MoS2 sheets and abundant carboxyl groups of CDs introduce a high density of negatively charged sites. These electrostatically attract dissolved cations like Ca2+ while allowing benign interaction with carbonate ions due to favorable polar interactions [56]. Both constituents also introduce redox-active sites which catalyze antisolvent supersaturation control via H2O2 generation from O2 reduction while regenerating membrane hydrophilicity [57].

We thus postulate a dynamic combination of physicochemical and catalytically-induced effects stemming from the rationally designed CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite in deterring crystallization fouling from complex scaling ions under high salinity conditions. This multifunctional coating platform circumvents limitations of single-parameter approaches by synergistically harnessing emergent two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenide properties to elicit a targeted antifouling response unexplored thus far.

The excellent antifouling performance of the CDs@ MS-NF composite coating can be attributed to the synergistic interplay between altered membrane physicochemical properties and inherent functionality [58]. Increased surface roughness promotes hydrodynamic drag near the membrane-solution interface, hindering diffusion and crystallization kinetics of scaling ions. This effect supplements the favorable wettability change, where enhanced hydrophilicity due to quantum dot decoration hydrates mineral ions obstructing scale deposition. Therefore, we assume that the targeted CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite platform deter crystal adhesion through interlinked (i) physicochemical alterations of membrane roughness, wettability and charge density as well as (ii) dynamic chemical/electrocatalytic mitigation of scale nucleating precursors. Further studies probing the specific roles of the coating constituents would provide deeper insights into these synergistic antifouling mechanisms.

This work demonstrates the exceptional ability of an integrated CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite coating to mitigate mineral scale formation and reduce membrane fouling under environmentally relevant scaling conditions. The composite was fabricated using a facile solvothermal approach and uniform deposition onto membrane surfaces was realized with an in-situ growth technique.

Systematic materials characterization verified the successful modification of key membrane parameters like surface roughness, charge, wettability, and hydrophilicity with the nanoengineered coating. Short-term static fouling experiments exhibited a 3-fold lower flux decline along with significantly reduced calcite crystal adhesion for the coated membrane. This enhanced antifouling performance was attributed to synergistic mechanisms involving changes in surface properties and inherent catalytic activity of the nanocomposite constituents.

The excellent long-term scaling resistance capability of the CDs@ MS-NF layer was demonstrated through 30-day fouling experiments under mixed CaSO4/CaCO3 conditions. A mere 15 % flux drop was maintained compared to 60 % for the control membrane after 30 days. Detailed autopsies revealed lower surface coverage and elemental foulant ratios on the coated membrane, complemented by fouling resistance modelling. The development of multifunctional nanocomposite coatings for membrane fouling mitigation aligns with the growing interest in leveraging nanomaterials and advanced surface engineering techniques to enhance the performance and sustainability of membrane-based water treatment processes

The multifunctional CDs@ MS-NF nanocomposite platform provides a promising surface engineering route to circumvent precipitation fouling issues plaguing membrane desalination systems. This pioneering work is expected to open new avenues for exploiting emerging two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenides materials for next-generation antifouling membrane development.

Mahmoud F. Mubarak: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Heba Magdy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ghada E. Khedr: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation.
